headline: Ketel Marte Was Otherworldly, the Dragons Survived, and Week 9 Reminded Us Why We Play the Games
subheadline: A single player nearly carried an entire franchise, a pitching masterpiece went unrewarded in Wonder Wharf, and the title race is starting to take a shape that deserves your full attention.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

### Athletic Park Dragons def. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs, 5-4

There is a particular kind of loss that haunts fantasy managers through the long Tuesday morning after, when the final categories have been tallied and the math has been done and you sit there thinking: we were better. We were clearly better. How did we lose?

That is precisely where the **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** find themselves this week, staring at a 4-5 defeat to the **Athletic Park Dragons** that felt, by almost every measurable standard, like a result that should have gone the other way. Yoshinobu Yamamoto was magnificent, posting a 1.29 ERA and a 0.93 WHIP in a performance that would have been the unambiguous pitching highlight of most weeks in this league. Mitch Keller was solid. The Wonderdogs threw a league-high 79.2 innings and generated 69 strikeouts, second in the league only to Albuquerque. They out-pitched the Dragons. They out-strikeout-ed the Dragons. They threw 36 more innings than their opponent.

And still, the Dragons escaped. Brandon Wood contributed, Willson Contreras contributed, Jarren Duran contributed, Matt Clement contributed. It was not that the Dragons were great; it was that they were balanced and present and just good enough in enough categories to squeak across the threshold.

The Wonderdogs are 3-4-2 now, hovering at the precise edge of the playoff picture, doing enough to almost win and not quite enough to actually win. The Dragons remain firmly in the upper tier. But wonder what might have been, **Wonder Wharf**. You probably should.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

### Ketel Marte and the Week That Was

Here is the thing about Ketel Marte's week: numbers can describe it, but they cannot fully explain it. Eleven runs scored. Twelve RBI. Three home runs. A.563 on-base percentage. Those are the numbers, and the numbers are absurd, and yet even reading them in sequence does not quite prepare you for the reality of what happened, which is that one player essentially picked up an entire fantasy franchise and carried it, the way a worker bee somehow lifts something many times its own weight through nothing more than sheer biological insistence.

**BaseballPro77** won their matchup against the **Sugar Land Skeeters** 6-3, and Marte was the reason. The Skeeters were not exactly cooperative victims. Chase Burns and Michael Wacha both posted 1.50 ERAs. David Weathers contributed quality innings. This was, in other words, a genuinely good pitching staff doing genuinely good pitching things, and it still was not enough, because Marte was doing something that transcended ordinary category-by-category competition.

Gavin Williams added to the cause with a 0.50 WHIP that would have qualified as the stat of the week in almost any other context.

The Skeeters remain the team with the most cumulative points in the league (56 points for, most of any team), and a one-week stumble to a performance like Marte's carries no shame. But BaseballPro77 is now 8-1-0, and the distance between first and third in this league is narrowing in ways that suggest the real race is only now beginning to declare itself.

---

## Matchups

### The Upset: Benchwarmers def. Abbotsford-Colby Twins, 5-2

The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** hit 11 home runs this week. Eleven. They posted a.372 on-base percentage, which was the highest in the entire league. Their hitters were genuinely excellent and generated real, tangible offensive production. And they lost 5-2 to the **Benchwarmers**, which tells you almost everything you need to know about the structural problem at the heart of this roster.

The pitching gave it all back and then some. Zero NSVH. A 3.96 ERA in only 52.1 innings is not catastrophic on its own, but when combined with a complete absence of saves and holds, it creates a category floor that even the best offensive performance cannot fully compensate for. The Benchwarmers, meanwhile, had Casey Schmitt break out with 3 home runs and 8 RBI, Zach Neto added 3 home runs of his own, and the pitching posted a 2.83 ERA across 57.1 innings. This was the most complete and convincing win the Benchwarmers have put together all season, and the Twins are left wondering how they hit eleven home runs and still lost by three categories.

### Notable: Albuquerque Bombers def. Canberra Wombats, 8-2

The **Albuquerque Bombers** led the league this week in runs (45), strikeouts (70), stolen bases (8), quality starts (5), and NSVH (9). That is not a coincidence; that is a roster operating in genuine synchrony. Kyle Harrison posted a 0.43 WHIP across 7 innings, which is the kind of line that makes you check whether the box score is correct. Max Meyer added 14 strikeouts. The **Canberra Wombats** had Dylan Cease with 17 strikeouts and Christian Walker with 3 home runs, and it simply did not matter. The Wombats are now 0-7-2 and have been blown out in consecutive weeks.

### Notable: General Hospital def. North Correa, 8-1

The **General Hospital** victory over **North Correa** deserves attention precisely because it was so comprehensive. General Hospital posted the league's best ERA this week at 2.34 and a 1.04 WHIP. Eight categories to one is a decisive statement from a team that had been mired in a confusing mid-season skid, and the result suggests this roster has more in reserve than their recent record implied. Meanwhile, North Correa has a lineup that includes Shohei Ohtani and Randy Arozarena, starting pitching from Chris Sale and Marcus Severino, and they could only win one category. The pattern of underperforming in big spots is no longer a one-week anomaly for this team.

### Quick Takes

**Riverside Roughnecks def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 6-4.** The Roughnecks won, but the manner of the win (more on this below) keeps this one from feeling especially impressive. The Reapers threw only 40.1 innings from their rotation, which is catastrophically low, and Juan Soto's 4 home runs and 7 RBI in a losing effort is almost a metaphor for this entire franchise right now.

**Buttville Skidmarks def. Hiroshima Radiation, 6-2.** The Skidmarks won their matchup, but Hiroshima did them the considerable favor of posting a.233 OBP and only 1 home run despite having Bryce Harper on their roster. Winning 6-2 against that particular offensive performance is not an endorsement of the Skidmarks so much as an indictment of the Radiation, and both teams probably know it.

---

## Standings Watch

The shape of this race is starting to clarify, and what is clarifying is both exciting and a little ruthless, depending on where your team sits.

At the top, **BaseballPro77** at 8-1-0 has now separated themselves in the standings in a way that matters. They have more wins than any team in the league, and after Ketel Marte's historic individual week, they are playing the best baseball of any roster right now. The **Riverside Roughnecks** at 7-1-1 remain right behind them, and the **Sugar Land Skeeters** at 6-3-0 have the most cumulative points for in the league despite sitting third. These three teams feel like a genuine tier.

Below them, the **Athletic Park Dragons** at 6-2-1 and the **Albuquerque Bombers** at 5-3-1 represent a second tier with legitimate playoff credentials. The Bombers in particular look like a team that has found its footing.

Then there is the bubble, and the bubble is crowded and anxious. The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** and the **Benchwarmers** are both 4-5-0 and tied for points. **General Hospital** at 3-3-3 has the worst record math of any team in the running, but their performance this week suggests the ties may have been masking a better team than their record showed. The **Hiroshima Radiation** at 3-5-1 and the **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** at 3-4-2 are both still alive, but the window is narrowing with every week.

Below that line: the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** at 3-6-0 and **North Correa** at 2-6-1 face steep climbs. And **Canberra** at 0-7-2 is in territory that polite company does not usually discuss.

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

The **Riverside Roughnecks** are this week's lucky team, and the evidence is hard to argue with. They won 6-4 over the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** despite a.299 OBP, only 4 home runs, and Shane McClanahan posting a 7.20 ERA. Nick Kurtz's RBI production and a couple of pitchers who managed to avoid allowing earned runs over limited innings held the thing together, but the luck delta of plus-2.42 is the highest in the league this week. The Roughnecks are a genuinely good team, but this particular win arrived more as a gift than an achievement.

The **Buttville Skidmarks** take the unlucky designation this week, which requires some nuance given that they actually won 6-2. The issue is that their underlying production, solid pitching, nine NSVH, and respectable hitting, consistently generates fewer actual category wins than the numbers suggest it should. A luck delta of minus-1.35, the worst in the league this week, means the Skidmarks are leaving wins on the table in some structural way that is difficult to fix through roster moves alone. Winning against a historically weak Hiroshima offense does not change that underlying math.

---

## Waiver Wire

**[D] Hiroshima Radiation** dropped Matt Chapman, Colt Keith, Kyle Karros, and Colin Rea while adding Zack Gelof, Gavin Sheets, and J.T. Ginn in the most dramatic roster purge of the week. Ginn did show up in the box score with 14 strikeouts, which softens the blow slightly, but losing Chapman and Keith in one move swaps known production for speculative upside on a team that is already in freefall. Wholesale roster overhauls mid-season rarely produce the results that inspire them.

**[B+] General Hospital** added Jackson Holliday and dropped Jared Koenig, which is precisely the kind of low-cost, high-ceiling move that separates thoughtful managers from reactive ones. Holliday brings multi-position eligibility and genuine upside to a middle infield that has been inconsistent all year, and the timing, right after a convincing 8-1 win, suggests this is a team building confidence and depth simultaneously.

**[B-] BaseballPro77** added Ryne Nelson and Nick Lodolo while dropping Juan Morillo and Brayan Bello. The churning through SP options shows aggressive management of a rotation that is already producing well, and adding controllable starters to back an already functional staff is smart depth-building even when the individual names are not marquee additions. The execution was a little choppy, with Lodolo bouncing in and out of the wire, but the instinct is sound.

**[C+] Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** replaced Brenton Doyle with Garrett Mitchell in essentially a lateral outfield swap, then added Martin Perez to a rotation that threw only 40.1 innings this week. Mitchell and Doyle represent a push in value terms, and Perez is a workable innings-eater rather than a transformative add. But a team with a catastrophically short rotation needed to do something at starter, and Perez at least shows up and takes the ball.

**[C] Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** added Taylor Clarke, Jose A. Ferrer, and Jakob Marsee while dropping Erik Miller and Sean Newcomb. Three additions suggest urgency, particularly in the bullpen and outfield, but none of these names inspire significant confidence. Adding relievers to a team that already led the league in innings pitched this week is addressing a symptom rather than a cause, and the Wonderdogs' real problem is winning close matchups, not accumulating pitching volume.

**[C] Albuquerque Bombers** swapped Trevor Rogers for Grant Taylor in a quiet, low-stakes move that neither helps nor hurts a rotation that just had its best week of the season. Neither pitcher meaningfully changes the outlook for a staff that already posted a 2.82 ERA and 70 strikeouts, so this reads more as roster hygiene than genuine improvement.

---

## Trade Value Watch

**Ketel Marte:** His week was almost impossibly good: 11 runs, 3 home runs, 12 RBI,.563 OBP. A single week this extraordinary inflates perceived value, and managers around the league are now almost certainly overvaluing him relative to what his sustainable baseline looks like. **Sell.** If you own Marte, the next 48 hours represent a rare window to extract maximum value while the Week 9 glow is still warm.

**Yoshinobu Yamamoto:** A 1.29 ERA and 0.93 WHIP in a losing effort is the kind of line that makes other managers forget the loss and focus on the performance. Yamamoto is a genuine ace and the Wonderdogs' most valuable asset, but his value is highest to teams chasing pitching categories in the playoff stretch. **Hold** if you are in contention, but understand that his trade ceiling may not get higher than this.

**Nick Kurtz:** He keeps producing RBI even in weeks when the surrounding roster is not generating much offense. His role in the Roughnecks' unlikely 6-4 win illustrates a player who is quietly consistent in ways that the box score captures and the advanced metrics confirm. **Buy** if you can get him at a discount from a manager distracted by the Roughnecks' luck-inflated record.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **Sugar Land Skeeters** Lost to BaseballPro77 but pitching was excellent, with Burns, Wacha, and Weathers all shining, and the depth of this roster remains unmatched; a one-week stumble does not move the needle at the top. [DOWN]
2. **BaseballPro77** Ketel Marte's 11-run, 12-RBI,.563 OBP week was the most dominant individual performance of the season and Gavin Williams posted a 0.50 WHIP; this team is playing its best baseball of the year at exactly the right time. [UP]
3. **Riverside Roughnecks** A 6-4 win built more on luck (plus-2.42 delta) than dominance is a yellow flag, with only 4 HR and McClanahan's 7.20 ERA as real concerns, but Nick Kurtz's continued production and a deep bullpen keep them firmly in the top tier. [SAME]
4. **Albuquerque Bombers** An 8-2 demolition of the Wombats backed by Harrison's 0.43 WHIP, Meyer's 14 Ks, and balanced offensive contributions across the lineup is the most complete performance this team has put together in weeks. [UP]
5. **Athletic Park Dragons** Squeaking out a 5-4 win when the opponent out-pitched you is a credit to a balanced lineup, with Wood, Contreras, Duran, and Clement all contributing, but the rotation's low innings total (43.0 IP) remains a structural vulnerability. [SAME]
6. **Benchwarmers** Casey Schmitt's breakout (3 HR, 8 RBI) and Zach Neto's 3 HR gave this offense a genuine power identity it has not shown all season; the pitching was solid enough to hold, and this is the most convincing win the Benchwarmers have posted. [UP]
7. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** Yamamoto's 1.29 ERA, Keller's solid outing, and a league-high 79.2 innings pitched is an impressive pitching performance that deserved better than a 4-5 loss; this team keeps doing enough to almost win but not enough to actually win. [SAME]
8. **General Hospital** An 8-1 blowout of North Correa with balanced contributions across the lineup and a 2.34 ERA suggests this team is healthier than their recent skid implied; the Jackson Holliday addition on the wire is an upside play worth watching. [UP]
9. **North Correa** Getting blown out 8-1 despite Ohtani, Arozarena, and genuine pitching talent from Sale and Severino is a baffling result; the offense simply did not show up, and the pattern of underperforming in big spots is becoming a defining trait. [DOWN]
10. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** A 5-2 loss despite 11 HR and a.372 OBP is maddening, because the offense is legitimately good, but zero NSVH and a 3.96 ERA in only 52.1 innings means the pitching continues to give back everything the bats produce. [SAME]
11. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** Juan Soto's 4 HR and 7 RBI in a losing effort is the most Wisconsin Rapids Reapers outcome possible; only 1 QS and 40.1 innings pitched is catastrophically low and this rotation cannot sustain a playoff push if it keeps falling apart. [DOWN]
12. **Hiroshima Radiation** A 6-2 loss despite 7 NSVH, a 3.38 ERA, and 72.0 innings pitched is the most damning offensive indictment of the season: 33 hits, 1 HR, and a.233 OBP from a lineup that includes Bryce Harper is simply not acceptable. [SAME]
13. **Buttville Skidmarks** A 6-2 win over Hiroshima is welcome but hardly inspiring; the offense is still generating too few hits and the luck delta of minus-1.35 suggests even this win came against a historically weak opponent. [UP]
14. **Canberra Wombats** Dylan Cease's 17 Ks, Seth Lugo's 2 QS, and Christian Walker's 3 HR were not nearly enough to overcome an 8.31 ERA from the rest of the staff; this team is in freefall and has now been blown out twice in consecutive weeks. [SAME]

---

## Looking Ahead

Three matchups in Week 10 deserve your full attention.

First: **Albuquerque Bombers vs. Riverside Roughnecks** is the week's marquee game, a battle between the league's third and fourth-ranked teams in the standings that will almost certainly clarify the shape of the top tier. Albuquerque is playing its best baseball of the season. The Roughnecks need to prove their 6-4 win was real and not just the luck delta talking.

Second: **Hiroshima Radiation vs. Sugar Land Skeeters** is a matchup with genuine stakes for both teams, though in very different ways. The Skeeters need a convincing bounce-back after losing to BaseballPro77, and Hiroshima needs to prove their hitters can actually hit. This one could go badly for the Radiation.

Third: **BaseballPro77 vs. Buttville Skidmarks** will tell us whether the league's hottest team can sustain their momentum against a roster that, despite its record, has some genuine pitching depth. If BaseballPro77 wins this one going away, the league is going to have to start taking their title chances very seriously indeed.
